name: What happens if a handyman finds hidden damage during a simple repair?

mainEntity.name: How should homeowners handle unexpected damage discovered after opening a wall, cabinet, or exterior surface?
mainEntity.text: How should homeowners handle unexpected damage discovered after opening a wall, cabinet, or exterior surface?

acceptedAnswer.text: Hidden damage is common when a handyman opens a wall, cabinet, ceiling, or exterior surface. What looks like a small repair may reveal rot, mold, water intrusion, loose framing, or damaged wiring or plumbing. The right response is to pause the job, document the issue, and explain how it changes the scope before proceeding. Some discoveries can still be handled as part of a handyman repair if they are minor and non-structural, but larger problems may require a different plan, additional materials, or another specialist. Homeowners should expect the estimate to change if the work expands, because labor and material needs are no longer the same as the original task. This is a useful unanswered question because many people want to know what happens in the middle of a repair and whether they will be surprised by extra charges. A strong service page should explain the process clearly so customers know how scope changes are handled.
acceptedAnswer.description: If hidden damage is found, the handyman should stop and explain the issue before continuing. Small cosmetic repair may still be possible, but rot, mold, structural damage, or active leaks can change the scope and cost. A photo-based update and revised estimate are usually the next step.
